items.reality: Node is a runtime that executes JavaScript (and, after compilation, TypeScript) outside the browser using the V8 engine.
items.example: JavaScript runs in 3 main places: the browser (V8/JavaScriptCore/SpiderMonkey), Node (V8 + libuv + Node's stdlib), and runtimes like Deno/Bun (different stdlibs). The language is JS; Node is one host.

items.reality: Your JavaScript runs on a single main thread with an event loop; parallelism needs worker threads or child processes (libuv handles some I/O in a thread pool).
items.example: Two HTTP requests arriving simultaneously are *interleaved* on one thread — Node schedules them around `await` points. True parallel CPU work needs `worker_threads`, `child_process`, or scaling horizontally.

items.myth: ESM and CommonJS are the same.
items.reality: They differ in syntax and loading semantics; mixing them requires care, and __dirname (CJS) has no direct ESM equivalent (use import.meta.url).
items.example: ESM equivalent of CJS `__dirname`: `const __dirname = path.dirname(fileURLToPath(import.meta.url))`. ESM imports are also async-aware; CJS `require` is synchronous.
